text: Hello and welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Oba from Talabat. Welcome,
Oba. Hi, Mo. Everyone in the Middle East knows Talabat, but for those who don't know,
can you tell us a bit more about Talabat? So Talabat is the biggest food delivery
platform in the MENA region. We don't just only deliver food, we deliver grocery, pharmaceutical,
flour and so much more. Recently we launched a Talabat match. We deliver groceries 24-7 in less
than 30 minutes. That's amazing. I will enjoy ordering the groceries from the comfort of my
couch. You brought an interesting architecture today. Can you tell us a bit about the challenges
and problems you're trying to solve with this architecture? So two years ago we started our
digital transformation journey because we wanted to serve customers better and actually innovate a
lot more. This is one of the reasons why we migrated. We started looking at migrating our services from
our on-premise to AWS by doing something we called like our re-architecting of our applications
to EKS and at the same time using serverless called Lambda. Excellent. So you looked at your applications
and then some of them fall in the category of containers, some of them fall in the serverless category.
Absolutely. Because we allowed to give our development team the flexibility. Yeah.
Not just only that, we looked at the database as well. One of the things we did was we started extracting
our database from our proprietary SQL database to Aurora. Yeah. So you had the proprietary databases on
premise and you moved to Aurora. What was the benefit of using Aurora? So one of the benefits of using Aurora is because it's open source.
It's a managed service that provides us infinite scalability. Excellent. So I see here there's a router. What is the role of the router?

When we started this migration journey, because Talabat is so big and critical and because we serve over 500,000 orders per day, it will be much more important that we provide the highest resilience to our customer.
So one of the things we did was we did something called like a weighted traffic shifting between our hosted data center to EKS and Lambda.
Yeah. Okay. So you used it for gradually migrating the traffic between the workloads on on premise and the services that we have on AWS.
Absolutely. And that's one of the things that the router did was to actually provide us this resiliency.
Amazing. So I see here is Beanstalk and EC2. What are they used for?
So when we started this migration journey two years ago, then like last year, COVID happened. During this COVID, we could not serve a customer faster as much as we wanted to.
So one of the things we thought was we needed to accelerate this migration journey by doing something we called like a re-platforming of our application to Beanstalk.
And not just only that, one of the things we did was we implemented something called like a lift and shift of our database to EC2.
Yeah. So I see that there was categories of applications that you moved to EKS and Lambda using the re-architecting technique.
And there is other categories of applications you moved to Beanstalk using the re-platforming technique, right?
Yes, because the applications that were migrated to EKS and Lambda were less complex applications and it doesn't take us a lot of time to understand the internals.
But applications that were moving to Beanstalk would take us a lot of time to actually understand how it works and to actually provide like the higher scalability.
And because we're already hitting the limit of how much we can serve our customer, we needed to just do something that we call re-platforming.
And Beanstalk provides this because we do not have to spend a lot of time to understand how to migrate, I mean, how to scale properly.
Excellent. So you move faster by re-platforming the workloads.
What is the future plans for you with the Beanstalk and the self-hosted database on EC2?
So one of our big plans is actually to actually migrate all these applications in Beanstalk to Lambda and at the same time to Kubernetes.
With this, we can provide like a scalable platform for our customers.
Amazing. So overall, what was the benefits of the architecture that you see?
Unlike our on-premise data center, we can actually scale infinitely on AWS, provide better customer experience and innovate a lot faster.
Amazing. Thank you, Oba, for sharing this architecture with us today.
Thank you for having me, Mo.
Pleasure. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
